coredata.dc:title: Investigating the structural, dielectric, optical and photocatalytic properties of carbon dots and palladium, platinum decorated carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Bibi, Sheema
Shakir, Namra
Sadiq, Muhammad
Sadiq, Saima
Ullah, Inam
Khan, Qaisar
Bostan, Bushra
Ismail, Muhammad
coredata.dc:description: In this study carbon dots (CDs), palladium and platinum decorated carbon dots (Pd-CDs, Pt-CDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal method. The synthesized materials were characterized by Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM), X-ray Diffractometer (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), Photoluminescence (PL) and impedance analyzer. Photoluminescence study revealed that the CDs, Pd-CDs and Pt-CDs were fluorescent in nature. A comparative study was conducted to investigate the energy storage capacity of the different materials. The photocatalytic properties of the materials were studied through the degradation of Acid Red 4(AR-4) a member of azo dyes. For better photocatalysis optimized reaction parameters were concentration; 10 ppm, temperature; 328 K, time; 120 min and catalyst amount; 0.12 g/20 ml. Photocatalysis for Pt-CDs was observed superior than Pd-CDs and CDs. The degradation of AR-4 over Pt-CDs follows first order kinetics with rate constant of 2.2 × 10−3 min−1. Both experimental and theoretical findings revealed that the Pt decorated CDs have high catalytic activity due to its low band gap energy 1.235 eV as compared to CDs and Pd decorated CDs. Catalysts used for photodegradation exhibited exquisite regeneration capacity with minimal loss in activity.

head.ce:title: Investigating the structural, dielectric, optical and photocatalytic properties of carbon dots and palladium, platinum decorated carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study carbon dots (CDs), palladium and platinum decorated carbon dots (Pd-CDs, Pt-CDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal method. The synthesized materials were characterized by Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM), X-ray Diffractometer (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), Photoluminescence (PL) and impedance analyzer. Photoluminescence study revealed that the CDs, Pd-CDs and Pt-CDs were fluorescent in nature. A comparative study was conducted to investigate the energy storage capacity of the different materials. The photocatalytic properties of the materials were studied through the degradation of Acid Red 4(AR-4) a member of azo dyes. For better photocatalysis optimized reaction parameters were concentration; 10 ppm, temperature; 328 K, time; 120 min and catalyst amount; 0.12 g/20 ml. Photocatalysis for Pt-CDs was observed superior than Pd-CDs and CDs. The degradation of AR-4 over Pt-CDs follows first order kinetics with rate constant of 2.2 × 10−3 min−1. Both experimental and theoretical findings revealed that the Pt decorated CDs have high catalytic activity due to its low band gap energy 1.235 eV as compared to CDs and Pd decorated CDs. Catalysts used for photodegradation exhibited exquisite regeneration capacity with minimal loss in activity.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a new carbon-based nanomaterial that have attracted attention in the previous decade due to their unique physio-chemical, structural, optical, and electrical properties. As a result, CDs have emerged as a promising field, for a range of health, environmental and energy storage applications including cell imaging, degradation of pharmaceuticals, pH sensing, metal ions detection, batteries, capacitors, dielectric, mechanical and catalytic materials [1–7]. In sensor optical or piezo effect detection like conductive layer and photoluminescence (PL) switching of quantum dots are main charge carrier layers [8]. Therefore, it is necessary to optimize the optical, dielectric and electrical properties by inserting conducting fillers such as C-based nanomaterials CNTs, CDs, or C-black [9–12].

CDs are the core investigated materials among semiconductor-based Q-dots with high fluorescence quantum yield, resistance to easiness of bio conjugation, photo bleaching, less toxicity and cost effective synthesis [13]. Until now, several procedures have been demonstrated for CDs synthesis like laser ablation, microwave irradiation, electrochemical carbonization, chemical ablation and hydrothermal method [14,15]. Hydrothermal method is the most common route among these approaches due to its simplicity, high yield, simple manipulation, low air pollution, convenience and economic benefits [16,17]. Numerous standard instrumental analytical techniques are employed for exploring different features of CDs e.g. X-ray Diffractometry, Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy, RAMAN spectroscopy, PL and impedance analysis spectroscopy [18]. Recent study showed the tendency towards optimizing and exploring CDs-based dielectric materials e.g. Chen et al. [19] reported dielectric behaviors of CDs and poly- methyl methacrylate nano composites. Carbon-based nano-composite has shown applications in solar cells, optical sensors [20], light emitting diodes, capacitors, and radio frequency electronics [21]. Moreover, from impedance analysis the dielectric behavior like an imaginary and real parts of the permittivity, dielectric loss, tangent loss, absolute admittance, capacitance, resistance and Q-values are calculated [22,23]. Recent literature shows that metal/non-metal nanocomposites of CDs has gained much attention. Jian et al. [24] fabricated CDs nanocomposites with polypyrrole as efficient and controllable electrode by electro-polymerization technique and it played a supporting role in process of electro-polymerization. Cyclic voltammetry, galvanic-static charge-discharge and electrical impedance spectroscopy techniques are employed to analyze the super capacitor characteristic features of metal/polypyrrole decorated CDs nanocomposites. The pure CDs have shown superior dielectric performance compared with metal-decorated CDs showing the tremendous candidate for energy storage devices. CDs retained their optical properties when decorated with metals and dielectric investigations confirmed the observance of relaxation process and the interaction of metals with host CDs [25].

The photocatalytic efficiency of the CDs can be enhanced by the establishment of a smooth and fast channels for electron transport, accelerate electron transport and prevent the recombination of electron-hole pairs [26]. Moreover, different metals have a synergistic effect on the photocatalytic activity of CDs i.e. titanium, tungsten (VI), silver, cadmium, zinc and copper etc. [27,28] [29–31]. The use of CDs as photocatalyst has been investigated for the degradation of various pollutants and organic wastes especially dyes, antibiotics and pesticides [32–34]. Hao et al. [35]. reported the synthesis of carbon dots modified MIL-101(Fe) octahedrons and successfully utilized it for the degradation of persulfate(PS)-activated tetracycline(TC) antibiotic. The CDs anchored on rod-like CoFe2O4 was utilized for the efficient removal of amoxicillin [36]. The photothermal effect of CDs can be exploited for the photothermal assisted splitting of water [37,38]. The CDs act as photocatalyst and effectively degrade dyes as reported in literature. De et al. [39] reported the synthesis of Cu2OCDs hybrid nanocomposites for the catalytic oxidation of ethyl paraoxon under sun light by UV spectroscopy. The photodegradation of methyl orange dye was conducted in aqueous solution by N-doped and undoped CDs and H2O2. The N-doped CDs showed catalytic activity up to 90 % in a reaction time of 120 min under xenon lamp as light source. The activity of un doped CDs was much lower (31 %) as compared to N-doped showing that N doped CDs are superior catalyst and the catalytic activity of CDs increases with doping [40]. Similarly, the degradation of Rhodamine B dye by N and SN doped CDs (quantum) has been reported to 78 and 71 % respectively [41]. Aziz and coworkers utilized CDs for the elimination of rhodamine B dye from waste water [42]. These promising results by CDs provokes us to design CDs based novel catalyst for the degradation of azo dyes.

In the recent study, we have investigated dielectric, optical and catalytic properties of the synthesized CDs, palladium and platinum decorated CDs. The metals Pd and Pt was opted in this study due to its versatility, stability, selectivity, compatibility, high regeneration capacity and their ability to decrease band gap and increase light absorption which in turn enhance photocatalytic activity [43]. Band gap energies have been simulated for the confirmation of enhanced photocatalytic properties of decorated CDs against the degradation of azo dyes. The materials have excellent use in electro-optics applications as well as in photo-catalysis.

Chemicals and reagents of high grade were used in current study such as d-glucose, urea, platinum chloride, palladium chloride, ethanol and Acid Red 4. All the chemicals were used without any further treatment.

Carbon Dots and metal decorated CDs were synthesized using the autoclave instrument (WAC80, Wittig Germany). Structural characteristics of the samples were investigated by Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) and X-rays Diffractometry (XRD) while spectrofluorometric studies were conducted by using a spectro-fluorophotometer (model: RF-5301 PC, Shimadzu, Japan). However, RAMAN and FTIR spectroscopy were performed through (Raman microscope, Renishwaw, UK) and FTIR spectrophotometer, Shimadzu, Japan respectively. UV–Visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-1800) was used for tracing the degradation of dye.

The synthesis of Carbon dots (CDs) were conducted through a hydrothermal route by dissolving 0.4 g of urea and 0.25 g of d-glucose in 80 mL of double distilled water. The resulting mixture was sonicated for 20 min and subsequently autoclaved for 10 h at 120 °C, resulting in a reddish brown solution. The mixture was again sonicated (20 min) for particle dispersion which is followed by centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 20 min in order to separate the CDs nanoparticles. The obtained CDs were subsequently dried in oven for two days.
The fluorescence spectra of the CDs were then measured by scanning the aqueous solution in the range of 200–700 nm, revealing that emission and excitation occurred at 600 and 358 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Pd and Pt decorated CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A small amount of palladium and platinum (precursor salts: 0.01 g) were added to the mixture of glucose and urea. This mixture was then sonicated and autoclaved for different time intervals of 10 to 14 h. The resulting particles were dispersed in ethanol. The solution was then centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 20 min. The upper clear part of the solution was discarded and the remaining sediment was dried as shown in Fig. 1 .

The carbon dots are small parts of graphene with 0D (quasi-zero-dimensional) and ideal CDs are present in only single atomic layer. In current study, CDs consist of 7 hexagonal rings and 36 atoms and were modeled in single layer. The edges of structure are saturated with 12 H-atom to avoid the edges effects. The optimizations were done in a tetragonal super cell of 12 × 12 × 20 and preferred large vacuumed space in Z-direction to avoid above image interaction as shown in Fig. 2 . For all simulation we used GGA [44] and PBE as exchange functional while DNP was employed as a basis set [45,46]. The 1 × 1 × 1 k mesh point grids were chosen for optimization with global orbital cutoff 4.5 A°. Also, for Van der Waals effect correction employing Grimme's DFT-D technique [47].

The blank test for the azo dye was conducted by running the reaction without catalyst and hydrogen peroxide which lead to no degradation. After this the reaction was conducted without catalyst in the presence of H2O2 and again no degradation was observed. The degradation was observed only when the catalyst, hydrogen peroxide and dye solution was present in the reaction mixture, whereas degradation efficiency was found to be dependent over temperature, pH, catalyst amount and time of the reaction under UV irradiation.

The stock solution 100 ppm of Acid Red 4 dye was prepared and then dilution formula was used to prepare different concentrations for degradation reactions. The effect of different parameters like pH, temperature, catalyst loading, dye concentration and time were studied and optimized. To investigate the catalytic activity, the optimized reaction conditions i.e. 10 ppm (20 mL) solution of dye, 0.12 g catalyst, 1 mL hydrogen peroxide at pH 5 were taken in three necked modified Pyrex glass reactor at 55 °C and stirred (600 rpm) for 120 min under UV irradiation. The flux of the source UV lamp was determined by using high pressure mercury lamp HPL-N 250 W (chromaticity coordinate-381) or actinometer: potassium oxalate-ferrioxalate and found to be 1.25 × 1017 hv/s where window glass was used as a filter. The degradation was measured from the absorbance peak measured by UV–Visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-1800) at regular intervals of time. The following formula was used to calculate the percent degradation of the dye at various conditions and parameters.
%
d e g r a d a t i o n =
(
C i − C t
)
C i
X
100
Where Ci is the initial concentration of dye and Ct is the final concentration of dye.

The amount of organic contents in the treated waste water after degradation experiment was calculated by chemical oxygen demand (COD). The COD quantify the organic content to be 120 mg/L which is in the range of permissible limits issued by environment protection agency (EPA) [48].

TEM images of CDs, Pd and Pt decorated CDs are presented in Fig. 3 (a)–(c), respectively, which provide a clue about the cubic crystal shape of all the nanoparticles. The particles are uniformly distributed over the surface and show small and uniform particle size. The observed particle size for Pd and Pt nanocrystals were in range of 7–11 nm. Furthermore, there is no agglomeration or cluster formation in both the Pd and Pt decorated CDs. XRD patterns (Fig. 3(d)) were recorded from 15° to 80° showing intense peaks at 2θ = 18.3°, 22.9°, 37.6°, 43.0°, 64.3° and 78.5°. The diffracted peak exhibited at 2θ= 22.9° was obtained for synthesized CDs and it is well indexed with JCPDS26-1076 of graphite, which confirmed their poor crystalline nature [49,50]. Previous studies [51,52] have reported that the peak at 22° of CDs synthesized by a hydrothermal method is due to the presence of O and N. Similarly, the peak observed at 18.3° resembled the crystal plane (002) of CDs [53]. Additionally, The synthesized composite materials of CDs shows (311), (220), (111) and (200) crystal planes and are in accordance with preceding results [54]. The crystal planes observed in the Pd and Pt-CDs were also consistent with literature findings. Photo luminescence (PL) of palladium and platinum decorated CDs synthesized by hydrothermal method is shown in Fig. 4 (a). The excitation was observed at wavelength of 315 nm while strong emission bands were appeared in the range of 320–332 nm which shows the fluorescent nature of CDs. Fluorescence emission intensity of CDs gradually rises up to 325 nm and then slowly declines at 330 nm.

Peaks in the scattered light's wavelength and intensity were determined by the Raman spectrum (Fig. 4b) and each peak corresponds to particular anomalous molecular bond oscillation, including vibrations of single bonds like Carbon-Carbon, Carbon-Hydrogen, and Nitrogen-Oxygen as well as groups of bonds like the benzene ring, lattice and polymer chain. The region of 125–3500 cm−1 is typically occupied by the Raman spectra. Initially, single bond absorption occurs in the range of 2500–4000 cm−1 and in between 2503 and 1995 cm−1, several bands (-Nitrogen=Carbon=Oxygen) absorb wavelengths and double bond absorbs between 1500 and 2000 cm−1 (-C=O, -C=N). Many molecules below 1500 cm−1 show complex patterns of C-N and C-C oscillation. Bands less than 655 cm−1 are caused by some lattice or inorganic group's vibration. Spectrum of CDs prepared through hydrothermal route, as presented in Fig. 4(b), show two primary bands and G band is represented by the broad band at 1576 cm−1, while the D band is expressed by the shoulder peak at 1366 cm−1. In general, the D band is connected to the vibrational state of a carbon atom that has experienced sp [3] hybridization, referred to a disordered band, whereas the G band is connected to a CD that has experienced sp [2] hybridization, referred to a graphitic nature band. The letters ID and IG, respectively, stand for the ardencies or intensities of the D and G bands. The modest graphitic structure of synthesized CDs was characterized by the low intensity ratio of the D to G band (ID & IG) [49,55]. The Raman spectra of CDs decorated with Pd and Pt displayed characteristic absorption bands of C-nano crystals at 1356 cm−1 (D band) and 1586 cm−1 (G band). This suggests that the intense fluorescence of CDs in Pd-CDs and Pt-CDs experienced partial suppression due to photo-induced electron transfer from the CDs shells to the palladium or platinum cores. This indicates that the CDs were effectively coated with palladium and platinum nanomaterials [56]. The bending and stretching modes of oscillations of the FTIR peaks identify the various functional groups present on the surface of Pd and Pt decorated CDs as shown in Fig. 4 (c & d). The FTIR spectrum of the synthesized CDs confirms a distinctive peak intensity at 3257 cm−1, which corresponds to stretching vibrations of N-H. The weak absorption at 2919 cm−1 attributed to vibrations caused by carbon-hydrogen stretching. The COO-asymmetric and symmetric groups stretching vibrations can be detected in the range of 1400–1580 cm−1. The existence of the C=O band is responsible for absorption at 1715 cm−1 and the presence of the C=C bond is responsible for the peak at 1634 cm−1. The functional group stated above makes CDs water soluble.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dielectric properties of CDs, Pd and Pt decorated CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The dielectric characteristic features e.g. dielectric constant, tangent loss, dielectric loss etc. were calculated by impedance spectroscopy at room temperature with the frequency range 1–3 GHz. Relative permittivity can be expressed by Eq. (1).
(1)
ε
′
= Cd / A
ε
o
Where ε', d, C, A, and εo” represents relative permittivity, thickness of Pellet, capacitance, cross sectional area and permittivity of free space, respectively.

The Fig. 5 (a) displays the variation of ε' in the frequency range of 1–3 GHz. It is clear that the dielectric constant exhibits a significant variation with frequency. ε' appears to remain constant in the range of 1-2 GHz, but starts to decrease in the 1.3–1.6 GHz region. Subsequently, there is a sudden increase in the dielectric constant, which reaches to its maximum value at a frequency of 1.8004 GHz. Then, the dielectric constant starts to decrease again but shows a subsequent increase in the 2.2–2.4 GHz region. The effect of the addition of Pd and Pt on CDs is noteworthy. The CDs exhibit a maximum dielectric constant value of 69.65 in the frequency region of 1.6–2.5 GHz, compared to Pd-CDs (4.7) and Pt-CDs (14.47). The changes observed in the relative permittivity with frequency are due to the formation of space-charge polarization at the interface between the electrolyte and electrode. These observations confirmed the non-Debye type of behavior [57,58].

Fig. 5(b) depicts the variation in the dielectric loss factor (ε'') with frequency. It was observed that the value of ε'' remained constant in the range of 1-2 GHz. However, it is decreased in the frequency region of 1.3–1.6 GHz and showed a sudden drop, attaining its minimum value at a frequency of 1.785 GHz which then passes through a maximum dielectric loss factor of 11 at 1.82 GHZ. ε'' touches the maximum value of 11 once again at 2.5 GHZ followed by successive decrease. The effect of addition Pd and Pt to CDs showed that the CDs obtained the minimum dielectric loss value (-54.43), as compared to Pd-CDs (-0.78) and Pt-CDs (-6.49), in the frequency range of 1.6–2.5 GHz. This variation is attributed to the rotation of the COOR functional group linked to CDs [59], indicating the beta relaxation of the material. Our result are consistent with that reported literature by Logakis et al. [60]. The beta relaxation was observed in the less frequency region and can be attributed to the opposing rotation of COOR side groups connected to the material. The loss tangent is the current loss ratio to charging current of material and given by the Eq. (2)
(2)
Tan
( δ )
=
ε
″
/
ε
′
Where ε" and ε' shows dielectric loss factor and dielectric constant, respectively.

The graph in Fig. 5(c) shows the relationship between tangent loss and frequency. It can be observed that the minimum tangent loss occurs at lower frequencies, while it becomes constant at higher frequencies due to reduced polarization at greater external field strengths. This was described in a previous study [61]. In particular, the graph shows that the tangent loss remains constant within the range of 1-2 GHz, reduces between 1.3 and 1.6 GHz and reaches to a minimum at 1.725 GHz and then becomes constant again in the range of 2.2–3 GHz.

Furthermore, the incorporation of Pd and Pt into the CDs resulted in some variations. The CDs had the lowest tangent loss value (-158.007) in the 1.6–2.5 GHz frequency range compared to Pd-CDs (−0.80) and Pt-CDs (−6.7). The quality factor is the inverse of tangent loss, commonly referred to as the dissipation factor. The materials' quality factor remained steady between 1 and 1.27 GHz, then quickly increased and resonance took place between 1.27 and 1.36 GHz. After resonance, the negative peak is also developed in the frequency range of 1.38 GHz–2.92 GHz Fig. 5(d). The increase in Q-values is due to the decrease of dielectric loss and increase of relative permittivity and the peaks noticed when the external filed f matches with the hooping f and the negative peaks noticed due to pores nature of material [62]. The impedance of a component refers to its resistance in a circuit at a specific frequency. It measures the degree to which a circuit resists the flow of electricity [63]. Resistance causes some amount of energy to be converted into heat, and minimizes current flow [64]. To calculate the impedance of a capacitor, Eq. (3) is used which is given as;
(3)
Xc =
1
2 π f C

Where Xc is the impedance in unit ohms, f is the frequency of the signal passing through the capacitor, and C is the capacitance of the capacitor. Now, the magnitude (absolute value) of Z is given by:
(4)
| Z |
=
R
2

X L − X C
)

Initially, the absolute impedance values of CDs, Pt-CDs, and Pd-CDs were observed to decrease smoothly up to 105 MHz and remain constant on further frequency increase. CDs showed lower impedance values compared to Pt-CDs and Pd-CDs at 1 MHz as shown in Fig. 5(e). The impedance phase initially displayed a slow decrease up to 1.54 GHz, with a sudden decrease giving negative resonance peak values for all materials in the frequency range of 1.57–1.75 GHz. It then started increasing and exhibit positive resonance peak values in the frequency range of 2.28–2.70 GHz. At lower and higher frequency regions, CDs displayed maximum and minimum impedance phase values respectively Fig. 5(f) as compared to Pt-CDs and Pd-CDs.

The resonance resistance (R) of CDs, Pt-CDs, and Pd-CDs Fig. 6 (a) were observed to be -4543.45, -4604.22, and 2543.73, respectively, at 1 MHz. The R-value of CDs and Pt-CDs increased, and the R-value of Pd-CDs decreased up to the 15.99 MHz frequency, displaying a constant behavior on further frequency increase. The R-value refers to the internal resistance of a circuit consisting of an ideal capacitor and resistor, where useful energy is lost as undesirable heat. It appears in series with the capacitance of the device and ranges from a few milliohms to several ohms [65]. Equivalent series resistance (ESR) values range from a few milliohms to several ohms and result in reduced efficiency, power losses, and instability of regulator circuits and power supplies [66]. The X-value Fig. 6(b) of CDs, Pt-CDs, and Pd-CDs were −24,665.67, −33,709.39, and −49,410.28, respectively, at 1 MHz. The X-value rapidly increased up to the 135.57 MHz frequency and showed slow increases on further frequency increase. CDs displayed the maximum value of equivalent series resistance compared to Pt and Pd-CDs. Admittance measures how easily a material can allow the flow of current through it and is defined as the reciprocal of its impedance [67]. It is represented by following formula:
(5)
Y =
1 Z

Here, “Z” is impedance and “Y” is admittance. [45] The absolute admittance showed constant behavior up to the 1.60 GHz frequency, then suddenly gave a resonance peak, declined, and gave a second resonance peak in the frequency range of 1.67–2.65 GHz. CDs showed high values of absolute admittance (0.3391) compared to Pt-CDs (0.0747) and Pd-CDs (0.0295) in the frequency range of 1.6–1.8 GHz. Furthermore, in the higher frequency range of 2.2–2.5 GHz, CDs, Pt-CDs, and Pd-CDs displayed absolute admittance values of 0.1522, 0.0775, and 0.0419, respectively as shown in Fig. 6(c). Capacitors are said to be in parallel when the two terminals of one capacitor are connected to each terminal of another capacitor, with all connected capacitors having the same voltage [68].

For n number of capacitors in parallel, we can write the equivalent capacitance as
(6)

∑ 1 n
C i = C 1 + C 2 + C 3 + …

The equivalent parallel capacitance (Cp) showed constant behavior up to the 2.0 GHz frequency, then suddenly gave a resonance peak, declined, and gave a second resonance peak in the frequency range of 1.68–2.59 GHz as shown in Fig. 6(d). At 1.66–1.89 GHz, CDs, Pt-CDs, and Pd-CDs displayed Cp values of 2.59 × 10−11, 7.02 × 10−12, and 2.67 × 10−12 respectively, with respective Cp values of 9.53 × 10−12, 6.12 × 10−12, and 2.94 × 10−12 in the higher frequency range of 2.26–2.47 GHz. CDs displayed the highest value of Cp among the three materials. Equivalent series capacitance is defined as the total capacitance for a series combination of capacitors [69]. It is represented as:

For n number of capacitors connected in series the equivalent capacitance will be;

This is represented by an equation. The Cs plot displays Fig. 6(e) a constant behavior up to a frequency of 1.680 GHz. After that, there is a resonance peak at 1.72 GHz, followed by a decline, and another constant behavior in the frequency range of 1.81–3 GHz. The Cs values for CDs, Pt-CDs, and Pd-CDs are 4.081 × 10−9, 1.72 × 10−9, and 1.67 × 10−9 respectively, for the frequency range of 1.65–1.81 GHz. It is evident from the figure that the CDs have a higher Cs value compared to Pt-CDs and Pd-CDs. The reciprocal of reactance is denoted mathematically as 1/X. It was previously measured in mhos but is now measured in Siemens. The equivalent parallel susceptance (B) shows Fig. 6(f) a constant behavior up to a frequency of 1.5 GHz. There is a resonance peak followed by a decline and a second resonance peak in the frequency range of 1.6–2.5 GHz. The B values for CDs, Pt-CDs, and Pd-CDs are 0.29, 0.074, and 0.029 respectively, in the frequency range of 1.65–1.9 GHz. Furthermore, in the higher frequency range of 2.22–2.49 GHz, CDs, Pt-CDs, and Pd-CDs exhibit B values of 0.141, 0.082, and 0.041, respectively. The Fig. 6 shows that CDs have the highest B values compared to Pt-CDs and Pd-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity of carbon dots based catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activity of the synthesized CDs, Pd-CDs and Pt-CDs were investigated for the degradation of an azo dye i.e. Acid Red 4 (AR-4). As the Pt-CDs are the most efficient among the catalysts, so considered further for the degradation of the AR-4. The photodegradation was found to be dependent over time, catalyst amount, dye concentration, temperature and pH [70]. The various parameters for the efficient photocatalytic activity of Pt-CDs were optimized for the degradation of AR-4 which are given in Fig. 7 (a–f). The effect of time in the degradation AR-4 was observed from 20 to 120 min representing a percent degradation of 80 to 100 %, respectively. The degradation increases with the increase in time as shown in Fig. 7(a). The effect of amount of catalyst was investigated from 0.04 to 0.2 g and observed that the degradation efficiency increases up to 0.12 g but after that it decreases as shown in Fig. 7(b). The maximum degradation of 100 % was achieved at 0.12 g while further increase in catalysts dose results in the declining of the% degradation. The degradation decreases at higher catalyst dose due to the agglomeration of catalysts. Additionally, dye interaction initially increases to the active sites of catalyst which results in the maximum degradation at 0.12 g catalyst and then decreases due to the competitive adsorption as the catalyst amount were further increased beyond the optimum amount [71]. The effect of dye concentration was investigated in the range of 10–50 ppm which shows that the% degradation decreases as the concentration of the substrate increases as given in Fig. 7(c). The most probable reason for the decrease in catalytic activity at higher dye concentration of Pt-CDs can be attributed to the little number of active sites over the surface of catalyst as compared to substrate molecules [72]. The similar results were also observed by Neppolian et al. [73] in the degradation of Reactive Yellow 17(RY17), Reactive Red 2(RR2) and Reactive Blue 4 (RB4) dyes where a successive decrease in the percent degradation of these dyes occurs with increase in concentration. This behavior could be explained as at low concentrations; the catalyst is able to absorb more photons thus results in the maximum degradation efficiency. Alternatively, at high concentrations, the path length of photons entering the solution reduces. The effect of temperature was monitored from 25 to 55 °C as shown in Fig. 7(d). The increase in temperature has a direct effect on the activity of the photocatalyst and 100 % degradation was observed at temperature > 50 °C which is due to the partial polarization of dye molecule with increase in temperature [74]. The pH effect was investigated from 2 to 11 and the effect in acidic and basic medium were quite different from each other as shown in Fig. 7(e). The catalyst exhibited more% degradation in the acidic range as compared to basic range. The decrease in the% degradation in the basic range shows the stability of the substrate at pH> 7. The reason is low ionization at basic region while high ionization (protonation) in acidic region. The catalyst showed 100 % degradation of the dye at pH ≈ 5 which is assumed due to the maximum protonation of the dye in the acidic range. The kinetic study of the degradation reaction was carried out by using the concentration and time parameters as shown in Fig. 7(f). The data shows that the degradation of acid red 4 dye follow first order kinetics with the regression of 0.9422 and rate constant of the reaction is 0.0022 min−1.

Fig. 8 (a) shows the comparative catalytic activity of CDs, Pd-CDs and Pt-CDs in the degradation of AR-4. As it is evident from the results that the Pt-CDs possess high% catalytic activity which is attributed to the synergistic effect of platinum on the synthesized photocatalytic material. The simulated band gap observed at Fig. 9 (a) for the synthesized CDs was 2.857 eV. This band gap is narrowed down by decorating CDs with Pd and Pt nanoparticles. The observed band gap for the Pt-CDs (1.235 eV) was much lower than individual CDs as well as Pd-CDs (2.027 eV). Thus synergistic effect of Pt-CDs was higher as compared to individual CDs and Pd-CDs. The photodegradation of azo dyes is a photo induced reaction in which charge carriers are the significant steps in the degradation reaction. An ideal situation can be observed for the material having high charge separation and broad absorption range. The charge separation of CDs is dependent over the band gap and particle size. The incorporation of Pt and Pd nanoparticles over the CDs causes the band gap reduction. The high reduction in band gap is caused for Pt-CDs which is attributed to the larger particle size of Pt as compared to Pd. The narrow band gap of Pt-CDs is responsible for the high catalytic activity and low band gap energy. The exposure to light energy source is the primary reason for the generation of charge carriers [62].

Moreover, the surface functionalization has also a key role in the adsorption of substrate to the active sites over the surface of CDs catalyst. The high adsorption is the significant point in the high catalytic efficiency of Pt-CDs as compared to the other catalysts [63].

Table 1 depicted the effectiveness of metal-CDs instead of CDs as photocatalysts which was elaborated here by theoretical simulation of band gap energy as shown in Fig. 9 (a, c, e). The simulated band gap energy of the The CDs, Pd-CDs and Pt-CDs was also compared with experimentally determined band gap energies exploiting tauc plot Fig. 9 (b, d, f). The same trend of decrease in the band gap energy was observed as metals Pd and Pt were incorporated in the synthesized CDs. Although the experimental values were in close agreement with theoretical results but a little bit of variation was observed which may be attributed due to some assumptions in the equation similarly, experimental conditions may also vary in terms of sample quality and measurements [75]. The incorporation of small amount of Pd and Pt are responsible for the photo excitation due to the decrease in band gap between the valance and conduction band and shift the reaction from higher energy to lower energy. The mechanism of the dye degradation can be illustrated from the Fig. 10 , showing the differences between valance and conduction bands for the CDs, Pd-CDs and Pt-CDs. It is assumed that the photocatalysis of the azo dye follows the same mechanism as reported in literature [76]. The electron under UV irradiation are transferred from valance to conduction band producing an e −/h + pair, where the h + interact to the H2O present at the surface of catalyst, generating H + and OH· radical. The dye combines with the OH· radical to generate water, carbon dioxide and nitric oxide. The generalized form of mechanism is represented in Fig. 10, showing the holes’ migration from CDs to active metal and the results in the redistribution of fermi energies. The simulated results show that there is high band gap between the valance and conduction bands of CDs, hence less catalytic activity is shown by the CDs. While in case of Pt-CDs, the possible conclusion for the enhanced photocatalytic activity is due to the decrease probability of the recombination of e −/h + as the Pt-CDs has 1.235 eV band gap energy difference that causes the enhancement in the catalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recycling and activity of recycled catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The recycling experiment for the synthesized catalysts were conducted against the degradation of Acid red 4 dye for five consecutive cycles under similar experimental conditions i.e. 10 ppm dye (20 mL) solution, time= 120 min, temperature= 55 °C, pH= 5, catalyst amount= 0.12 g under UV-irradiation. After each run the catalyst was recovered and then used for next reaction under similar reaction conditions. The process was repeated for five times. The percentage of degradation for the three catalysts were calculated and plotted against the no of cycles as shown in Fig. 11 . The maximum% degradation has been recorded for Pt-CDs followed by Pd-CDs, showing a very slight but gradual decrease in the degradation efficiency. This decrease was correlated to the loss in catalyst amount as a result of active metal leaching [83]. The stability of catalyst in term of leaching was observed in CDs while the activity shows a constant trend,

In current study carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized through hydrothermal route and then decorated with palladium and platinum. Various aspects of the synthesized materials were explored by different analytical tools such as TEM, XRD, Raman, FTIR, PL and impedance analyzer. The XRD results showed that the CDs along with Pd- CDs and Pt- CDs had a minimum degree of crystallinity and have an amorphous nature. Impedance analysis revealed that the CDs had the maximum dielectric constant value (69.65) compared to Pd-CDs (4.7) and Pt-CDs (14.47) in the frequency range of 1–3 GHz. Additionally, CDs showed the highest parallel capacitance and susceptance values compared to Pt-CDs and Pd-CDs, and demonstrated an improved Cs value in the frequency range of 1.65–1.81 GHz. Due to fluorescent nature CDs and Pd, Pt decorated CDs can be exploited as a probe for a variety of purposes. The photocatalytic activity of the catalyst was investigated in the photodegradation of AR-4 dye. Due to low band gap energy, Pt-CDs exhibited excellent performance in photocatalytic applications especially in the degradation of AR-4 dye (≈100 % degradation) as compared to CDs and Pd-CDs. These findings indicated that CDs have potential for various applications in energy, optical and biomedical fields. Overall, this study highlights the novel synthesis and characterization of CDs and their composite materials, and provides important insights into their properties and potential applications.
All the authors contributed equally

Sheema Bibi: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Namra Shakir: Investigation. Muhammad Sadiq: Supervision, Conceptualization. Saima Sadiq: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Inam Ullah: Writing – review & editing. Qaisar Khan: Writing – review & editing. Bushra Bostan: Writing – review & editing. Muhammad Ismail: Investigation.
